coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence enhancing and quenching signal based on new approach for selective detection of multiple organochlorine pesticides using blue emissive-carbon dot
coredata.dc:creator: Nethaji, P.
Revathi, P.
Senthil Kumar, P.
Logesh, M.
Rajabathar, Jothi Ramalingam
Al-Lohedan, H.A.
Arokiyaraj, S.
Rangasamy, Gayathri
coredata.dc:description: Measuring the concentration of organochlorine pesticides (OCPs) in agriculture has engaged significant awareness for healthcare investigation since OCPs are harmful to many physiological processes. Excessive usage of these compounds can result in major contamination of the environment and food supply chains. As a result, more accurate and rapid ways to detect pesticide residues in food are required. In this work, we have portrayed the surface-engineered fluorescent blue emissive-carbon dot (B-CD) with a high quantum yield (49.3 %) via the hydrothermal method for fluorescent sensing of OCPs on real samples. The amine group functionalities of carbon dots have supported the direct coordination with –Cl and –OH groups of HEP, ENS, CDF and 2,4-DPAC for the sensitive detection of OCPs, by switching in the fluorescent intensity of B-CD. The functional group of OCPs exhibits a variety of binding interactions with B-CD to contribute a complex formation, which leads to static quenching via an insubstantial restricted electron transfer process. The synthesized carbon dots exhibit individuality in binding nature towards different OCPs. Fluorescence studies help to distinguish the target OCPs and their low detection limits (LODs) were 0.002, 0.099, 0.16 and 0.082 μM for Heptachlor (HEP - turn “on”), Endosulfan (ENS), Chlordimeform (CDF) and 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-DPAC - turn “off”) OCPs respectively. The real water samples and agriculture food samples were effectively investigated and the OCP toxicity was noted. Thus, the design of the fluorescence sensor is established as an easy and proficient sensing method for detecting OCPs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: B-CD
Binding affinity
Fluorescence quenching
OCPs
Sensor
Biodegradable

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of HEP and 2, 4-DPAC OCPs
Sensing mechanism for the detection of HEP and 2, 4-DPAC OCPs
Sensitivity and selectivity of ENS, CDF and 2, 4-DPAC for OCPs
Detection of HEP, ENS, CDF and 2,4-DPAC in real water and food samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support through Researchers Supporting Project number ( RSP2024R354 ), King Saud university , Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a & b) TEM image, (c) SAED pattern and (d) Histogram plot of CD.
(a) XRD pattern and (b) FTIR spectrum of B-CD and (c) EDX spectrum of B-CD solution.
(a) UV spectrum, (b) Fluorescence spectra of CD solution, (c) UV light (365 nm) of CD solution and (d) PL emission peak positions are dependent of emission wavelength for the B-CD.

(a) Fluorescence spectra for CD with ENS at various concentrations (b) Resolution of fluorescence (c) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and ENS concentrations; (d) selectivity of ENS (5 μM) and coexisting pesticides (50 μM).

(a) Fluorescence spectra for CD with CDF at various concentrations (b) Resolution of fluorescence (c) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and CDF concentrations; (d) selectivity of CDF (10 μM) and coexisting pesticides (50 μM). The standard deviation for each of the three separate tests was shown in error bars.

(a) Fluorescence spectra for CD with 2,4-DPAC at various concentrations (b) Resolution of fluorescence against the 2,4-DPAC concentrations; (c) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and 2,4-DPAC concentrations; (d) selectivity of 2,4-DPAC (5 μM) and coexisting pesticides (50 μM). Error bars displayed the standard deviation for three individual tests.

(a) Fluorescence spectra for CD with HEP at various concentrations (b) Resolution of fluorescence (c) Linear between fluorescence of HEP concentrations; (d) selectivity of HEP (2 μM) and coexisting pesticides (20 μM). The standard deviation for three different tests was shown by error bars.
Structure of different OCPs we have used for study.
The interaction of B-CD with (a) HEP and (b) 2, 4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid is illustrated schematically.
Various CDs based fluorescence sensor for detection of pesticides.

row.entry: Tap water, distilled water, river water
Grape juice, tap water, CRP water
River water, tap water, rice, soil, serum and apple.
[Y. Hu and Z. Gao et al., 2020]
Pond water, River water, Tap water, Soybean sprouts and Mung bean sprout

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This paper has been recommended for acceptance by Charles Wong.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence enhancing and quenching signal based on new approach for selective detection of multiple organochlorine pesticides using blue emissive-carbon dot

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Measuring the concentration of organochlorine pesticides (OCPs) in agriculture has engaged significant awareness for healthcare investigation since OCPs are harmful to many physiological processes. Excessive usage of these compounds can result in major contamination of the environment and food supply chains. As a result, more accurate and rapid ways to detect pesticide residues in food are required. In this work, we have portrayed the surface-engineered fluorescent blue emissive-carbon dot (B-CD) with a high quantum yield (49.3 %) via the hydrothermal method for fluorescent sensing of OCPs on real samples. The amine group functionalities of carbon dots have supported the direct coordination with –Cl and –OH groups of HEP, ENS, CDF and 2,4-DPAC for the sensitive detection of OCPs, by switching in the fluorescent intensity of B-CD. The functional group of OCPs exhibits a variety of binding interactions with B-CD to contribute a complex formation, which leads to static quenching via an insubstantial restricted electron transfer process. The synthesized carbon dots exhibit individuality in binding nature towards different OCPs. Fluorescence studies help to distinguish the target OCPs and their low detection limits (LODs) were 0.002, 0.099, 0.16 and 0.082 μM for Heptachlor (HEP - turn “on”), Endosulfan (ENS), Chlordimeform (CDF) and 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-DPAC - turn “off”) OCPs respectively. The real water samples and agriculture food samples were effectively investigated and the OCP toxicity was noted. Thus, the design of the fluorescence sensor is established as an easy and proficient sensing method for detecting OCPs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organochlorine pesticides (OCPs) are extensively used worldwide as herbicides, fungicides, insecticides, rodenticides, and nematicides. Despite being prohibited due to their long-term toxicity, their residue has an impact on the quality of both primary and processed agricultural products. OCPs lead to contamination of the human body and the environment because of their persistence and excessive mobility. OCPs are widely utilized in agriculture to control insect pests on an extensive range of crops. Pests and diseases kill 30 % of agricultural production each year around the world. In today's agriculture, OCPs perform a dynamic role in the prevention of pests and diseases. Excessive usage of OCPs, on the flip side, results in widespread residual levels in water, food and the environment. One of the most recognized types of organic contaminants that persist are OCPs due to their widespread dispersion, resistance to degradation, and hazardous effects [C.C. Egbe et al., 2021]. Because most OCPs are lipophilic and hydrophobic, they are extremely resistant to environmental degradation and can last for years or decades after being used. As a result, groundwater, food items, surface water, soil, and air have all been affected. OCPs are sprayed in an unchecked manner due to a lack of knowledge about the exact amount of OCPs needed and a need to improve crop yield quickly. OCPs that fall into the organochlorine group, are incredibly dangerous. Because of the existence of chlorinated hydrocarbons, organochlorine OCPs are noted for their elevated toxicity and bioaccumulation [Moldovan, Rebeca, et al., 2021]. As a result, developing a sensitive and fast system to detect OCPs in food, soil, and aqueous medium is critical. A multiplicity of conventional OCP detection analytical methods are currently available, including chromatography, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, electrochemical methods, MS-HPLC and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELIAs). Simple fluorescence-based probes have recently been tested for OCP detection. These probes may be able to solve the problems associated with approaches that rely on expensive instrumentation due to their flexibility, ease of use, and low detection limits [Pooja, Sharma et al., 2020].

Carbon dots, a fascinating category of carbon-based nanomaterials, typically consist of a carbon-based nucleus and multiple surface functional groups. The B-CD has appealed a lot of consideration because of its distinctive characteristics, which include fluorescence [Hu, Rongjing, et al., 2020], electrochemiluminescence, catalysis, and optical catalysis. Since Sun Y.P. first suggested the concept of CD [Bhosale, Rajesh S. et al., 2017], multiple hybrid nanomaterials-based fluorescence sensors have received a lot of interest because of their impressive applications in chemistry and biology. Since of their great fluorescence strength, low cost, good water solubility, good conductivity, biocompatibility, photochemical stability, low toxicity, and environmental friendliness, the B-CD in particular are an especially promising substitute for metal-based mixed sensors. B-CD is attracting an increasing number of researchers who want to learn more about their applications in detection [K. Radhakrishnan et al., 2020]. B-CD are excellent candidates for chemical sensing, bioimaging, and optoelectronic applications because they are the most promising nanomaterials [Meng, Chen, et al., 2023]. A few researchers recently produced fluorescent B-CD derived from aloe for the selective identification of tartrazine in food samples such as sweets, steamed buns, and honey [Chatzimitakos, Theodoros, et al., 2017].

Currently, however, numerous research, including drug delivery, chemical sensing, photodynamic therapy, photocatalysis, biosensing, bioimaging, and electrocatalysis, have been conducted on the high-tech applications that are both versatile and promising of carbonic nanomaterials, as well as carbon quantum dots. Their superb optical characteristics and fluorescence emissions are drawing a lot of interest in the scientific community right now [Shi Ying Lim et al., 2015; Ding et al., 2014]. B-CD is considerably superior to quantum dots and fluorescent dyes as candidates. This is because, they have the least cost, low toxicity, biocompatibility, chemical inertness, simple synthesis procedures, and tunable spectrum of excitation and emissions [Krysmann, Marta J. et al., 2012; Liu, Meng Li, et al., 2019; Kong, Biao, et al., 2012; Zhou et al., 2012; Ray, S. C. et al., 2009; Sun, Ya-Ping, et al., 2006; Baker, et al., 2010; Dai, Haichao, et al., 2014]. The emissive traps, quantum effect and radioactive exciton recombination are thought to be the primary causes of CD's PL feature [Chandra, Sourov, et al., 2012]. Any variables that affect the HOMO, LUMO, and CD band distance can have an impact on its PL properties [Song, Yubin, et al., 2014]. Even so, the installation of different functionalities on the surface of the CD can create surface defects that further add to the B-CD' PL property [Yang, Yunhua, et al., 2012].

In this paper, a different fluorescence sensing established on B-CD for the multi-detection of OCPs was designed. This has the advantages of a low detection limit, a broad detection range, and good selectivity. In this work, the PL of the B-CD sensor is quenched by 2, 4-DPAC (off) and enhanced by HEP (on) as shown in Scheme 3(a & b). The B-CD was generated via a one-step hydrothermal treatment (Scheme 2) of OCPs in this investigation, and the created B-CD was utilized to detect Heptachlor (HEP), Endosulfan (ENS), Chlordimeform (CDF) and 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-DPAC) OCPs (Scheme 1
). The developed fluorescent-based sensor method is characterized by its sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, high reproducibility, suitability for real samples, and simplicity of execution. Furthermore, the test was carried out in real water and food samples to assess the accuracy of this sensing probe. The B-CD fluorescence increased after interacting with HEP, and in this case, the B-CD operated towards enhancing high HEP sensitivity with a LOD of 0.002 μM. In an aqueous solution, the B-CD' fluorescence decreased, acting as a quencher for these OCPs with a high sensitivity for 2,4-DPAC and a LOD of 0.082 μM. In addition, actual pond and tap samples were used to conduct an accuracy analysis of the nanoprobe, which yielded good outcomes. Furthermore, the findings showed that the suggested fluorescent-based sensor may be used to detect OCPs in real water samples.

The citric acid (≥99.5 %) and urea (≥99.5 %) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich. Merck's analytical grade gelatin was obtained from HEP, ENS, CDF and 2,4-DPAC from Sigma-Aldrich. The research studies used millipore-filtered water.

HR-TEM (JEOL/JEM 2100) established the particle size and morphology of B-CD at a working voltage of 2000 kV. The synthesized sample's UV–visible absorption spectra were calculated by spectrophotometers (Shimadzu UV-2600). Spectrofluorometers measured the excitation and emission rate of B-CD (HORIBA JOBIN YVON Fluoromax-4). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement using the X'pert powder diffractometer with Cu–K alpha radiation was used to analyze the crystalline and amorphous composition of the samples. FT-IR spectra were recorded with surface functionalization (Agilent Resolution Pro).

1 mmol of Citric acid (0.192 g) and 3 mmol of urea (0.184 g) were dissolved in distilled water (25 mL) in a typical procedure for preparing amine-functionalized B-CD. It was impenetrable in a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 6 h. The CD solution was treated with ethanol after cooling to 32 °C and then centrifuged for 10 min at 10000 rpm to extract the precipitate. The clear yellow solution dispersion was subjected to dialysis against water using a dialysis membrane for 24 h. This process was carried out to remove any unreacted ions and small particles. Finally, the resulting dispersion was vacuum-dried at 70 °C for 72 h. Polymer-like materials were the final items, and the maximum production yield was 67 % (Scheme 2) [Li, Xiaoming, et al., 2014].

Using B-CD, fluorescence quantum yield (QY) values for HEP, ENS, CDF, and 2, 4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid were determined using an aqueous solution of quinine sulphate (0.1 M H2SO4) as a reference dye, which has a QY of 54 % at 379 nm. The QYs of synthesized B-CD are calculated via the following equation (1):
(1)
Q x
=
Q std
(
I x
/
I std
)
I std
)
(
ɳ x 2
/
ɳ std 2
)
(
A std
/
A x
)
Here, Qx and Qstd are the QYs of the synthesized B-CD and quinine sulphate; ɳx 2 and ɳstd 2 are the refractive indices of H2SO4 and water; Ix and Istd are the integrated emission areas; and Ax and Astd are the optical densities of the synthesized B-CD and quinine sulphate dye. B-CD have been demonstrated to have a 49.3 % QY [K. Radhakrishnan et al., 2020].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of HEP and 2, 4-DPAC OCPs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized B-CD is used as an OCP detection and as a result, HEP and 2,4-DPAC were chosen as a model to assess the B-CD detection efficacy in this sample. The B-CD solution and ultrapure water were used as positive and negative controls, respectively, due to their high fluorescent emission. As a result, B-CD solution (0.1 mg ml−1) was applied to two target OCP concentrations. The fluorescence sensitivity of B-CD was measured in the presence of HEP (0–30 μM) and 2,4-DPAC (0–20 μM) at the indicated concentrations to determine enhancing and quenching efficacy. Then, using a spectrofluorometer (HORIBA JOBIN YVON Fluoromax-4) with a 4 nm slit width for both the excitation (372 nm) and emission (439 nm) peaks, the elevated fluorescence intensity of B-CD was quantified. Similar to that, the B-CD' selectivity was tested while the other interfering OCPs were present.

Real samples including pond, tap water, and river water served as research targets to verify practical applications of the fluorescence sensor. It's were collected from SRM University, Chennai, India. The Real samples (10 mL) were filtered through a 0.22 mm membrane. The spiked samples were mixed with 10.0 mL acetonitrile and subjected to ultrasound for 5 min. After centrifuging twice at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, the supernatant was collected. This was concentrated to 1 mL and diluted 10-fold to measure the HEP and 2, 4-DPAC according to the mentioned process. For the recovery study, certain amounts of HEP and 2, 4-DPAC standard (0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0 μg/L) were spiked into the samples before pretreatment and then fluorescence intensity was recorded. The distilled water analysis was conducted using the same actual sample analysis technique as previously mentioned.

The morphology and size were analyzed using HRTEM for the as-synthesized CD. The TEM image shown in Fig. 1 (a and d) displays its corresponding Gaussian-fit histogram plot and SAED pattern (Fig. 1c) [Carneiro, S. V. et al., 2019]. The dots are shown to be well distributed and have an average diameter of about 3 nm with a uniform size distribution shown in Fig. 1b. In Fig. 1, the HR-TEM image suggests the nature of both crystalline graphite and amorphous phases. First, the CD shown in Fig. 2 a was confirmed by the XRD study results. The large peak at 2θ = 21.1° corresponds to the graphite crystal lattice distance (002) (JCPDS Card No. 26–1076), referring to the increase in the carbon layer of sp2 [Shaikh, Asiya F. et al., 2019].

Using FT-IR and XPS, surface analysis was performed. An analysis of the samples was conducted to show the composition of the CD FT-IR. Numerous previous studies have shown that the compounds on the outer surface of the CD are due to their photophysical properties. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2b) of the as-prepared CD revealed the presence of similar functional groups in the gelatin moiety. The occurrence of an O–H and NH2-containing group was confirmed by an FT-IR peak at 3364 cm−1. The FT-IR peak at 1634 cm-1 suggested the existence of the stretching vibration of CO, indicating the presence of a carboxylic acid group. The appearance of the –NH group on the CD's surface was also indicated by the typical peaks of the stretching vibration at 1454 cm−1 [Panda, Snigdharani, et al., 2018]. EDX analysis was used to further investigate the elemental analysis of the produced B-CD. The presence of C, N and O in the B-CD was verified by the EDX spectrum displayed in Fig. 2c [De and Karak, 2013].

The XPS survey spectrum of B-CD displayed (Fig. 3 a) four typical peaks at carbon (282.4 eV), oxygen (530.3 eV), and nitrogen (396.12 eV). The O1s spectrum show (Fig. 3b) peak at 530.3 eV. As shown in Fig. 3c, the high-resolution C 1s were deconvoluted revealing the existence of four forms of carbon bonds: C–C (282.02 eV), C–N/C–O (284.03 eV), and CO (286.12 eV). Furthermore, the high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 3d) of N 1s was also analyzed to determine the binding energy of N–H, which was found to be 399.2 eV. Therefore, N 1s and C 1s spectra further confirmed that the amino groups were functionalized with the as-prepared CD. The FT-IR and XPS study indicated that certain amino groups were present on the surface of the CD's surface functional group [Cai, Huijuan, et al., 2021].

Fig. 4 a depicts the UV spectrum of the B-CD, which was recorded from 200 nm to 370 nm. The B-CD' absorption spectra exhibit a typical absorption peak at 282 nm, which is ascribed to the CC (aromatic) bond's and transition (π→π*). While the excitation wavelength for the fluorescence emission spectra of B-CD was 372 nm, the corresponding emission peak maxima were centered at 439 nm (Fig. 4b). Fig. 4c (inset image) illustrates how the B-CD aqueous solution seemed vivid blue under UV light (365 nm) [Bera and Mohapatra, 2020].

The fluorescence emission spectra of the B-CD are shown in Fig. 4d for various excitation wavelengths. From 310 to 470 nm were the range of excitation wavelengths, and between 433 and 461 nm were the peak intensities of the fluorescence emission. Peak intensity shifted to the red as the excitation wavelength was elevated. As a result, it was possible to deduce that the B-CD' fluorescence emission spectra were very sensitive to excitation wavelength. CD fluorescence characteristics are demonstrated by the fluorescence quantum yield (9.5 %) [Hou, Yue, et al., 2020].

For evaluating pH stability, the FL intensity of B-CD is collected to pH values in the range of 5.6–8.6. Fig. S1a (supporting information) revealed merely a change of B-CD, showing higher pH stability. Subsequently, thermal stability performance in Fig. S1b demonstrated that the B-CD holds almost constant under the temperature range from 5 to 93 °C. The chemical stability of different chemical substances was also investigated and explored. In Figure S1 (c-d), the normalized FL intensity was almost the same in the presence of NaCl (0–200 mmol L−1) and displayed a good tolerance ability towards H2O2 even in a high concentration of 5.0 mmol L−1, indicating satisfied chemical stability of B-CD. For photostability behavior, a time course scan curve of B-CD was obtained under 439 nm excitation for 60 min. Besides, an intensive xenon lamp was utilized under a continuous excitation of 40 min which provided the fluorescence intensity of B-CD. In Figure S2 (a-b), the fluorescence emission intensity of B-CD remained at approximately 83 % of the initial intensity after being exposed to xenon-lamp irradiation (17 W/cm2) for 40 min. This suggests that the B-CD exhibits excellent photostability [Basu and Mandal, 2019].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism for the detection of HEP and 2, 4-DPAC OCPs
ce:section.ce:para: The CD aqueous solution was subjected to HEP treatment, which likely facilitated the coordination of the highly reactive NH groups (Scheme 3a) with the amino group. There was probably minimal coordination with the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the B-CD, resulting in the disruption of the self-complexation between the B-CD molecules and their separation. This phenomenon increased the fluorescence strength of the B-CD, as well as a high binding affinity to the B-CD when compared to its non-detachable state. However, none of the carboxyl, amine, or OH group on the surface of the B-CD were included in the self-complexation. The other three OCPs, ENS, CDF, and 2, 4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid, may be subjected to ionic interactions, covalent binding and/or, H-bonding followed by a substitution reaction on the surface of the B-CD with the remaining available groups, resulting in CD fluorescence quenching without affecting its non-isolated state. Before the substitution process between these three OCPs, the chlorines in ENS, acting as leaving groups, may have effectively bound to the B-CD via covalent bonds [Li, Hongxia, et al., 2018]. However, the surface amine gave this OCP a higher binding affinity to the B-CD than HEP. On the other hand, the CDF's NH2 and N (element) in the benzene ring facilitated its ionic contact with the surface functions of the B-CD. As a result, these two OCPs had a lower binding affinity than the others. The difference in the affinities of these two towards B-CD, however, was due to the variation in the number of groups leaving the -Cl. However, due to the quenching of the lifetime of the B-CD in a small amount, there might have been a negligible occurrence of the electron transfer between the B-CD and the OCPs. The –COOH group is the reaction center for the interaction with CD with 2, 4-DPAC. The –COOH group of 2, 4-DPAC converts to COO− here, while the free primary amine group of CD converts to NH3+ and interacts with each other ionically (Scheme 3b). Hydrogen bonding interactions take place between the –COOH groups of 2, 4-DPAC and the –NH2 capped CD. Therefore, a higher binding constant of 2, 4-DPAC compared to CDF is demonstrated by both the ionic and hydrogen bonding interaction. As a result of the structural changes in the target OCPs, variations in the binding affinity for the B-CD were observed, suggesting that the as-synthesized B-CD is capable of detecting a variety of OCPs [W.-K. Li et al., 2020; Panda, Snigdharani, et al., 2018]. Then, every sample's time-resolved PL decay curve was also noted. All five samples' data can be fitted to dual exponential curves, as seen in Fig. S3 (Supporting information). It's an average lifetime of 1.70, 7.60, 9.96, 5.99 and 5.24 ns for the B-CD, HEP/B-CD, ENS/B-CD, CDF/B-CD and 2, 4-DPAC/B-CD, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of ENS, CDF and 2, 4-DPAC for OCPs
ce:section.ce:para: OCPs have long been used in agriculture, but their misuse has sparked widespread public concern about food safety and health protection. As a result, OCP detection that is both rapid and sensitive is essential. The excellent fluorescence sensing capabilities of B-CD prompted us to investigate the OCP detection applications. The sensitivity reply of the fluorescence sensor was studied for the several detections of ENS, CDF, and 2, 4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-DPAC) for OCPs with altered concentrations. The fluorescence quenching for BC-CD response to different concentrations of ENS, CDF & 2, 4-DPAC OCPs is shown in Figs. 5a, 6a and 7 a [Sharma, Pooja, et al., 2020].

We found that the fluorescence emission is quenched by ENS, CDF & 2,4-DPAC, but the other OCPs have no detectable influence on the fluorescence intensity, indicating that CD is very sensitive to ENS, CDF & 2,4-DPAC in DMF solution. Furthermore, the presence of other OCPs did not affect the luminescence quenching response generated by ENS, CDF & 2,4-DPAC in competition experiments, indicating that the CD might be a potential selective fluorescence sensor for ENS, CDF & 2,4-DPAC. Interestingly, the fluorescence of B-CD may be easily turned on by centrifuging the dispersion after the fluorescence quenching experiment and then washing with new DMF numerous times. After four recycling studies, the fluorescence intensity and quenching efficiency of B-CD remained essentially unaltered, as shown in Figs. 5a, 6a and 7a.

The fluorescence reduced expressively as the concentration of ENS, CDF & 2,4-DPAC OCPs increased from 0 to 21 μM, 0–25 μM, and 0–13 μM, respectively, as display in Figs. 5b, 6b and 7b. As a result, the concentration of fluorescence intensity increased from 0.02 to 0.1 μM (R2 = 0.998) for ENS (Fig. 5c), 0.4–2.8 μM (R2 = 0.992) for CDF (Fig. 6c) and 0.2–1.0 μM (R2 = 0.997) for 2, 4-DPAC (Fig. 7c). The LOD were found to be 0.099 μM for ENS, 0.16 μM for CDF, and 0.082 μM for 2, 4-DPAC which were obtained from three periods the standard deviation of the blank respectively. According to these findings, when compared to earlier studies on fluorescence sensors based on pesticides, this B-CD nanomaterial has exceptional high sensitivity, selectivity and quenching efficiency for ENS, CDF, and 2, 4-DPAC OCP detection. The advantages of the current sensing procedure were related with the formerly conveyed fluorescence sensors as shown in Table 1 . The findings indicate that B-CD exhibits favorable anti-interference properties and excellent recyclability for OCP sensing applications [Nazir, Kanwal, et al., 2020].

Fluorescence studies were carried out to better understand the CD's fluorescence emissive response to 2, 4-DPAC for a detection limit of 0.082 M, which is equivalent to the reported fluorescence sensor used for 2, 4-DPAC detection. To our knowledge, this is the first CD-based fluorescence sensing material that can detect trace levels of OCPs effectively. Other environmentally relevant poisons such as Tyr, Arg, Ser, uric acid, Phe, glutathione, Ala, His, glucose, cholesterol, lysine, gallic acid, and H-Cysteine (Figs. 5d, 6d and 7d) were used to test the suggested sensor's selectivity. One of these pesticides was typically applied to the CDs in an experiment at a final concentration of 20 min. Figs. 5d, 6d and 7d illustrate the fluorescence response at 200 μM for a 20-min reaction period. After incubation, a significantly higher selectivity and fluorescence intensity were observed for the target OCPs (ENS, CDF, and 2,4-DPAC) compared to other interfering pesticides. As a result, these target pesticides selectively responded to high catalytic activity, and the addition of co-interfering pesticides did not seem to have any noticeable effects. These findings showed that the suggested method had a respectable selectivity and could be utilized to successfully detect ENS, CDF, and 2, 4-DPAC [K. Radhakrishnan et al., 2019; Raja and Sundaramurthy, 2021].

The fluorescence sensor's sensitivity response was examined for the simultaneous detection of HEP, ENS, CDF, and 2,4-DPAC OCPs (Fig. 8 ). Fig. 8a and b shows the fluorescence spectra used to record the fluorescence sensor's (B-CD) sensing performance as HEP concentrations (0–19 μM) increased. With an increase in HEP content, the fluorescence intensity of the emission peak at 439 nm increased. The interaction of HEP (Cl) and the CD of the amine (NH2) group allowed scientists to identify the fluorescence emission.

The linear curve is displayed in the fluorescence emission spectra by plotting the CD's F/F0 at various HEP OCP concentrations. In the range of 1–19 μM HEP OCP, the fluorescence sensor exhibits a correlation coefficient of 0.998 (Fig. 8c). The experiments were performed in triplicate, and the detection limit was determined using formula 3 σ/K (where σ is the standard deviation of the measurement in the blank, and K is the slope of the plot). The detection limit for the HEP OCP was determined as 5.2 × 104 L mol−1.

As shown in Table 1, the outcomes were contrasted with those from the alternative methods. It is noteworthy to notice that the fluorescence sensor's CD probe exhibits an appropriate sensitivity to HEP OCP. The emission intensity ratios (F/F0 −1) for each OCP while keeping their concentrations at 3 × 10−5 M. The results depicted in Fig. 8a indicate that all the OCPs, except HEP, caused a reducing in the fluorescence intensity of the B-CD. The ENS had the largest reduction. However, when the HEP was added under similar experimental conditions, the B-CD showed an increase in intensity. These findings established that, relative to the other OCPs used in this analysis, the B-CD showed a strong selectivity towards HEP. It also indicated that the spectral shift of the B-CD was highest with the addition of ENS from CDF, 2, 4-DPAC and ENS. As a result, the B-CD can be used as a selective HEP OCP turn-on (Fig. 8d), as well as an excellent 2, 4-DPAC turn-off probe and an adequate test for the other OCPs that have been studied. The LOD for the four OCPs tested was also calculated using the following equation (2):
(2)
LOD = 3
( σ / Slope )

The slope is calculated as a function of OCP concentrations from fluorescence intensity at the limit, where σ is the standard deviation. The estimated LOD values for the four CD-OCP interactions are shown in Table 1. Table 1 also shows the least LODs of the four target OCPs as determined by diverse methods. Table 1 demonstrates that the detection method employed in this study yielded a low LOD for several OCPs, particularly HEP, which was comparable to the most recently discovered lowest LODs [Lin, Bixia, et al., 2018].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of HEP, ENS, CDF and 2,4-DPAC in real water and food samples
ce:section.ce:para: When used as an OCP sensor, the output of the proposed nanoprobe as-prepared CD was tested by detecting the HEP, ENS, CDF and 2, 4-DPAC in water and food samples. First, the water samples were tested using the traditional addition method to verify the consistency of the water samples. The water samples were obtained from our university and spiked with HEP, ENS, CDF and 2, 4-DPAC at different concentrations. Table (S1 to S4 Supporting information) indicates the revealing efficiencies of four OCPs in spiked water and food samples, with 92–99 % and 96.5–113.5 % recovery rates [Moldovan, Rebeca, et al., 2021]

The two OCPs' wide range of concentrations shows that this procedure was a reliable and appropriate way to identify OCPs in actual samples. In addition, the suggested techniques were compared to various previously published B-CD-based OCP detection methods. As shown in Table 1, compared to other LOD values. The water samples were replicated three times (n = 3), while the food samples were replicated five times (n = 5). As a result, the sensing methods could be used to monitor OCPs in real samples.

The ultra-low identification of various OCPs was performed via CD. Many of the OCP interactions with CD occurred in the excited state of CD. It has been discovered from steady-state measurements that HEP OCPs may bind to CD more strongly than, ENS, CDF and 2,4-DPAC. The presence of many good leaving groups (-Cl) in HEP was the possible explanation for their covalent CD binding, which can be replaced by the main CD amine group. B-CD-based sensing analytical techniques were developed and verified, and their application potentials were assessed for sensitive and selective detection of HEP, ENS, CDF, and 2, 4-DPAC residuals. Fluorescence studies based HEP at LOD value is 0.002 μM (“turn-on”) and 2, 4-DPAC at LOD value is 0.082 μM (“turn-off”). The R2 values obtained from the good linear plot are R2 = 0.998 and R2 = 0.992, respectively. The current effort offers a practical way to create fluorescence sensors that are both selective and sensitive for the visual detection of trace OCPs in actual water. Numerous applications exist for this type of design mechanism in the sensing of various pollutants in actual water. The B-CD has the potential to be used for sensitive and selective multiple OCP detection. In our future study, to widen its use for the identification of other OCPs, we will further enhance the discriminating potential of the B-CD-based sensor array.

P. Nethaji: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. P. Revathi: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. P. Senthil Kumar: Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation. M. Logesh: Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jothi Ramalingam Rajabathar: Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation. H.A. Al-Lohedan: Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation. S. Arokiyaraj: Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation. Gayathri Rangasamy: Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support through Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2024R354), King Saud university, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Raja and Sundaramurthy, 2021

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of fluorescent carbon quantum dots from Betel leafs (Piper betle) for Fe3+ sensing
Mater. Today: Proc.
